Mathematical definitions and conventions

Source: vignettes/website/kernels/mathematical-definitions-and-conventions.Rmd
mathematical-definitions-and-conventions.Rmd

Mathematical definitions and conventions

This vignette documents the probability distributions exported by DPmixGPD and the corresponding d/p/q/r functions used throughout the package. For a continuous random variable YY with parameter scalars 𝛉\boldsymbol{\theta}, we use the standard quartet:

  • Density (PDF): f(y∣𝛉)f(y\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}), implemented by d*().
  • Distribution (CDF): F(y∣𝛉)=Pr⁡(Y≤y)F(y\mid\boldsymbol{\theta})=\Pr(Y\le y), implemented by p*().
  • Quantile: Q(p∣𝛉)=inf⁡{y:F(y∣𝛉)≥p}Q(p\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \inf\{y: F(y\mid\boldsymbol{\theta})\ge p\}, implemented by q*().
  • Random generation: Y=Q(U∣𝛉)Y = Q(U\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}) with U∼Unif(0,1)U\sim \text{Unif}(0,1), implemented by r*().

All d*() functions optionally return log-densities via log = TRUE, i.e., log⁡f(y∣𝛉)\log f(y\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}). This is numerically convenient because likelihoods multiply densities but add log-densities: log⁡{∏i=1nf(yi∣𝛉)}=∑i=1nlog⁡f(yi∣𝛉). \log\Big\{\prod_{i=1}^n f(y_i\mid\boldsymbol{\theta})\Big\}=\sum_{i=1}^n \log f(y_i\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}).

All p*() functions support lower.tail and log.p: Pr⁡(Y>y)=1−F(y),log⁡F(y)orlog⁡{1−F(y)}. \Pr(Y>y)=1-F(y),\qquad \log F(y)\ \text{or}\ \log\{1-F(y)\}.

The d*(), p*(), and q*() functions accept vector inputs for their first argument and evaluate elementwise; r*() supports n > 1 (including n = 0).

w <- c(0.60, 0.40)
shape <- c(2.0, 5.0)
scale <- c(1.0, 2.0)

dGammaMix(seq(0.5, 2.0, length.out = 5), w = w, shape = shape, scale = scale)
[1] 0.182 0.219 0.216 0.194 0.165
qGammaMix(c(0.1, 0.5, 0.9), w = w, shape = shape, scale = scale)
[1]  0.731  3.126 12.550
rGammaMix(5, w = w, shape = shape, scale = scale)
[1] 0

Finite mixtures

Several kernels are available as finite mixtures. Let fj(⋅∣𝛉j)f_j(\cdot\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}_j) denote the jjth component density and let 𝐰=(w1,…,wJ)\boldsymbol{w}=(w_1,\ldots,w_J) be mixture weights with wj≥0w_j\ge 0 and ∑j=1Jwj=1\sum_{j=1}^J w_j=1. The mixture density and CDF are

f(y)=∑j=1Jwjfj(y∣𝛉j),F(y)=∑j=1JwjFj(y∣𝛉j). f(y)=\sum_{j=1}^J w_j f_j(y\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}_j), \qquad F(y)=\sum_{j=1}^J w_j F_j(y\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}_j).

Random generation proceeds by sampling a latent component index Z∼Categorical(𝐰)Z\sim\text{Categorical}(\boldsymbol{w}), then sampling Y∣Z=j∼fj(⋅∣𝛉j)Y\mid Z=j\sim f_j(\cdot\mid\boldsymbol{\theta}_j). Mixture quantiles typically require numerical inversion of the mixture CDF.

GPD tails and splicing

The Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD) is used for tail modeling relative to a threshold uu. In the spliced kernels (the *Gpd functions), a bulk kernel is used below uu and a GPD tail is attached above uu, with parameters (u,σ,ξ)(u,\sigma,\xi). In code, these are represented by threshold = u, tail_scale = \sigma, and tail_shape = \xi (the standalone GPD functions use scale and shape for σ\sigma and ξ\xi).

On this page

Developed by Arnab Aich.
